Title: Expression of the endocannabinoid system and response to cannabinoid components by the human fetal testis

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Endogenous cannabinoid signaling molecule potentially involved in fetal testis development
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Primarily Leydig cell-enriched interstitial areas of fetal testis
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected with no significant change from 6 to 17 DW
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports endogenous 2-AG biosynthetic capacity in human fetal testis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DAGLA transcripts are expressed in human fetal testis during 6–17 DW. 2. Expression remains relatively stable across development. 3. Presence of DAGLA is consistent with the high 2-AG levels detected in fetal testes.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Bulk RNA-seq; endocannabinoid quantification by mass spectrometry
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Gene was characterized as part of ECS background and not directly manipulated.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Endogenous cannabinoid signaling molecule potentially involved in fetal testis development
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Primarily Leydig cell-enriched interstitial areas of fetal testis
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected with no significant change from 6 to 17 DW
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports endogenous 2-AG biosynthetic capacity in human fetal testis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DAGLB transcripts are expressed in human fetal testis during 6–17 DW. 2. Expression is stable over developmental time examined. 3. Together with DAGLA it supports 2-AG production in fetal testis.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Background ECS enzyme; not functionally perturbed.

Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Bulk RNA-seq developmental dataset; endocannabinoid mass spectrometry
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Represents fetal testis AEA degradation capacity; increased expression accompanies developmental changes in AEA abundance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FAAH is expressed in human fetal testis and increases during development. 2. It indicates active AEA degradation machinery in fetal testes. 3. Its expression trend contrasts with increasing AEA content, suggesting alternative synthesis/degradation route complexity.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used to infer ECS metabolic dynamics rather than direct cannabinoid-treatment causality.

Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Bulk RNA-seq developmental dataset; endocannabinoid mass spectrometry
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports AEA synthetic capacity but declining expression suggests developmental remodeling of AEA biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NAT10 transcripts are present in fetal human testis. 2. Expression significantly declines across 6–17 DW. 3. The pattern, together with rising AEA levels, suggests alternative AEA synthetic routes may contribute in first trimester testes.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as a canonical AEA synthesis component.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Represents alternative 2-AG degradation machinery in fetal testis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABHD2 is expressed in human fetal testis and increases over development. 2. It is considered an alternative 2-AG degrading enzyme. 3. Authors suggest it may not be the primary 2-AG degradation route in fetal testis at this stage.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is discussed as part of ECS metabolic framework, not as a treatment-responsive core target.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: 7–17 developmental week fetal testes; treatment assays mainly 8–10 and 10–12 DW
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Male sexual differentiation and masculinization during fetal development
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leydig cells / fetal testis interstitial compartment
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 17-hydroxylated steroids and androgen precursors including DHEA/androstenedione pathway intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes 17α-hydroxylation and 17,20-lyase reactions that generate androgen precursors required for testosterone synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms essential precursor for target product
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point in androgen biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in fetal testis; significantly decreased after CBD/THC exposure in 10–12 DW explants
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected in human fetal testis transcriptome; downregulated after phytocannabinoid exposure
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Ex vivo exposure to CBD and/or THC for 72 h, especially CBD/THC mixture at 10^-5 M
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Bulk RNA-seq for developmental expression dynamics [previous dataset, accession not stated here]; 3' BRB-seq, GEO: GSE223827
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Downregulation is associated with reduced steroid biosynthesis and decreased testosterone secretion after phytocannabinoid exposure
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP17A1 was among steroid biosynthesis genes downregulated by phytocannabinoid treatment. 2. Its decrease was specifically highlighted after CBD/THC exposure in 10–12 DW fetal testis explants. 3. Reduced CYP17A1 expression supports the observed decline in testosterone production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: 3' BRB-seq differential expression; hormone measurement of testosterone in explant supernatants
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors note HSD3B1/2 and CYP11A1 were not altered transcriptionally, highlighting selective disruption within steroid pathway.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: 7–17 developmental week fetal testes; localization mainly assessed in 10–12 DW
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Steroid hormone / protein hormone / lipid signaling context
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Testosterone drives masculinization; AMH supports male reproductive tract differentiation; endocannabinoid signaling regulates fetal testis physiology
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leydig cells and testis cords context
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Endocannabinoid-responsive regulation of steroidogenesis, Sertoli cell function, cell proliferation and survival
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Likely mediates phytocannabinoid/endocannabinoid signaling affecting steroid biosynthesis genes including CYP17A1; direct target genes not experimentally proven
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Principal cannabinoid receptor expressed in fetal Leydig cells and germ cells, making it a plausible major mediator of cannabinoid effects on testosterone and testis development
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected from 6 to 17 DW; more abundant than CNR2; localized mainly in LIN28+ germ cells and CYP11A1+ Leydig cells, with low expression in AMH+ Sertoli cells
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: 2-AG; AEA; exogenous THC; modulated by CBD
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Human fetal testis ex vivo exposure to THC, CBD, or CBD/THC
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Bulk RNA-seq for developmental expression dynamics; RT-qPCR; RNAscope; 3' BRB-seq GEO: GSE223827
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides receptor basis for cannabinoid responsiveness in fetal testis and is implicated by inference in reduced testosterone/AMH secretion and altered cell survival after phytocannabinoid exposure
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CNR1 is expressed in human fetal testis from 6–17 DW. 2. RNAscope localized CNR1 mainly to Leydig cells and germ cells, with possible low Sertoli expression. 3. Its presence supports a direct mechanism for THC/CBD action on fetal testis physiology.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Bulk RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; RNAscope in situ hybridization with cell-type co-staining; ex vivo phytocannabinoid exposure phenotyping
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Protein detection by immunohistochemistry was unsuccessful due to nonspecific antibodies; role in phenotype is inferred rather than directly tested by antagonists/silencing.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: 7–17 developmental week fetal testes; localization mainly assessed in 10–12 DW
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Steroid hormone / protein hormone / lipid signaling context
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Testosterone drives masculinization; AMH supports male reproductive tract differentiation
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Endocannabinoid-responsive regulation of fetal testis cell function
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Secondary cannabinoid receptor present in fetal testis and a plausible contributor to phytocannabinoid responses, though less abundant than CNR1
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected from 6 to 17 DW without significant developmental change; occasional low-level signals in Leydig, germ and Sertoli cells
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: 2-AG; AEA; THC; CBD weak inverse agonism discussed
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Human fetal testis ex vivo exposure to THC, CBD, or CBD/THC
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Bulk RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; RNAscope; 3' BRB-seq GEO: GSE223827
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports the capacity of fetal testis cells to respond to cannabinoids; specific causal contribution to hormone/metabolite effects not directly resolved
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CNR2 transcripts are present in human fetal testis. 2. Expression is lower than CNR1 and only occasional signals were seen by RNAscope in major testicular cell types. 3. It may participate in cannabinoid-induced disruption of fetal testis physiology.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Bulk RNA-seq; RT-qPCR; RNAscope in situ hybridization
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific functional role was not directly tested; receptor protein not reliably detected by immunohistochemistry.
